sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 96, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. You may also `predict(expect_change=..., expected_grid=...)` before an `action(...)` to test your world model; `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats` then score it, and a `surprise` flags where your model was wrong -- optional, use it when you hold a belief worth checking.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 95 actions; a strong score needs about 54 or fewer. You are 1.8x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! After pressing UP:
- Cursor stayed at position 0 (rows 48-49, cols 15-19 is the top white bar, rows 59-60, cols 15-19 is the bottom white bar)
- Pink position 0 changed from cddd7d2c6a6ceea0 to 6610c6e38ccbd08b (cycled forward)
- Blue symbols changed! They were [a5a03333dcefae48, 598e9cbf8e60024f, 9fd5f2ec9386bc10, bb9796c6c98e1001, 8e4cb671a12a4302] and now they are [9fd5f2ec9386bc10, bb9796c6c98e1001, 8e4cb671a12a4302, ede50fceef9c1739, 19296f6e89610c96]
The blue symbols shifted! It looks like the blue row also cycles when I press UP/DOWN. Let me check:
Old blue: [a5a03333dcefae48, 598e9cbf8e60024f, 9fd5f2ec9386bc10, bb9796c6c98e1001, 8e4cb671a12a4302]
New blue: [9fd5f2ec9386bc10, bb9796c6c98e1001, 8e4cb671a12a4302, ede50fceef9c1739, 19296f6e89610c96]
It looks like the blue row shifted left by 2 positions, with new symbols appearing on the right. Or maybe each blue position also has a cycle, and pressing UP cycles all of them.
Wait, actually, looking more carefully:
Old blue[2] = 9fd5f2ec9386bc10 = New blue[0]
Old blue[3] = bb9796c6c98e1001 = New blue[1]
Old blue[4] = 8e4cb671a12a4302 = New blue[2]
So the blue row shifted left by 2 positions. The new symbols at positions 3 and 4 are ede50fceef9c1739 and 19296f6e89610c96, which are new.
Actually wait, maybe each blue position has its own cycle too. Let me check if the blue symbols are cycling independently.
Old blue: [a5a03333dcefae48, 598e9cbf8e60024f, 9fd5f2ec9386bc10, bb9796c6c98e1001, 8e4cb671a12a4302]
New blue: [9fd5f2ec9386bc10, bb9796c6c98e1001, 8e4cb671a12a4302, ede50fceef9c1739, 19296f6e89610c96]
If each blue position cycles independently:
- Blue[0]: a5a03333dcefae48 → 9fd5f2ec9386bc10
- Blue[1]: 598e9cbf8e60024f → bb9796c6c98e1001
- Blue[2]: 9fd5f2ec9386bc10 → 8e4cb671a12a4302
- Blue[3]: bb9796c6c98e1001 → ede50fceef9c1739
- Blue[4]: 8e4cb671a12a4302 → 19296f6e89610c96
So each blue position advanced by 1 step in its cycle. This means pressing UP cycles BOTH the pink position under the cursor AND all blue positions.
Wait, but the pink position only changed when the cursor was at position 0. Let me re-check:
Old pink[0] = cddd7d2c6a6ceea0, New pink[0] = 6610c6e38ccbd08b
In position 0's cycle: a2774f59f95f9997(0), b4edb3faf810133b(1), cddd7d2c6a6ceea0(2), 6610c6e38ccbd08b(3), 8b72057039a2be38(4), f4469c244897540c(5), 04a8e75f48884ca4(6)
cddd7d2c6a6ceea0(2) → 6610c6e38ccbd08b(3): yes, advanced by 1.
So pressing UP cycles the pink position under the cursor AND all blue positions. Pressing DOWN cycles them backward.
This changes everything! The blue row is also adjustable, and it cycles when I press UP/DOWN. The goal might be to match the blue and pink rows in some way.
Let me think about this. The blue row has 5 positions, each with a cycle. The pink row has 5 positions, each with a cycle. When I press UP/DOWN, the pink position under the cursor cycles, AND all blue positions cycle.
Wait, but that's weird. Why would pressing UP cycle all blue positions? Let me re-check by pressing UP again and seeing what happens.
Actually, maybe the blue row cycles independently of the cursor position. Let me test this by pressing UP again and seeing if the blue row cycles again.
But I've already spent 95 actions. Let me try to figure out the goal quickly.
Hypothesis: the goal is to match the blue and pink rows. When they match, the level completes.
Current blue: [9fd5f2ec9386bc10, bb9796c6c98e1001, 8e4cb671a12a4302, ede50fceef9c1739, 19296f6e89610c96]
Current pink: [6610c6e38ccbd08b, fb80c9b281b063da, b4edb3faf810133b, f4469c244897540c, b4edb3faf810133b]
These don't match. But maybe the goal is to make them match by cycling the pink positions.
Actually, wait. Let me re-read the blue row changes. The blue row changed when I pressed UP, but the cursor was at position 0. Maybe the blue row cycles when the cursor is at a specific position, or maybe it cycles every time UP/DOWN is pressed regardless of cursor position.
Let me test this by pressing UP again and checking if the blue row changes again.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Test if blue row cycles on every UP/DOWN press
# Current blue: [9fd5f2ec9386bc10, bb9796c6c98e1001, 8e4cb671a12a4302, ede50fceef9c1739, 19296f6e89610c96]
# Current pink: [6610c6e38ccbd08b, fb80c9b281b063da, b4edb3faf810133b, f4469c244897540c, b4edb3faf810133b]

action(['UP'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
blue = [(n['id'], n['hash']) for n in nodes if n['id'] in [44, 45, 46, 47, 48]]
pink = [(n['id'], n['hash']) for n in nodes if n['id'] in [49, 50, 51, 52, 53]]
print(f"Blue after UP: {blue}")
print(f"Pink after UP: {pink}")

# Press DOWN to reverse
action(['DOWN'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
blue2 = [(n['id'], n['hash']) for n in nodes if n['id'] in [44, 45, 46, 47, 48]]
pink2 = [(n['id'], n['hash']) for n in nodes if n['id'] in [49, 50, 51, 52, 53]]
print(f"Blue after DOWN: {blue2}")
print(f"Pink after DOWN: {pink2}")

# Check if blue/pink returned to previous state
print(f"Blue returned: {blue2 == [(44, '9fd5f2ec9386bc10'), (45, 'bb9796c6c98e1001'), (46, '8e4cb671a12a4302'), (47, 'ede50fceef9c1739'), (48, '19296f6e89610c96')]}")
print(f"Pink returned: {pink2 == [(49, '6610c6e38ccbd08b'), (50, 'fb80c9b281b063da'), (51, 'b4edb3faf810133b'), (52, 'f4469c244897540c'), (53, 'b4edb3faf810133b')]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
